summary: This notebook serves as a solution overview and index for a Kaggle competition entry focused on locating bacterial flagellar motors in 3D cryo-ET tomograms. It frames the volumetric detection problem as a 2D object detection task using YOLOv8, followed by 3D post-processing to consolidate predictions. Key takeaways include the effectiveness of percentile-based intensity normalization, the necessity of tomogram-level train/validation splits to prevent data leakage, and the use of 3D non-maximum suppression to cluster redundant slice-level detections. Readers learn how to adapt a standard 2D detector for specialized biological imaging and optimize inference for offline Kaggle environments.
competition_flow: Extracts 2D slices from 3D tomograms, normalizes intensity, trains a YOLOv8 detector on tomogram-level splits, and generates a submission by running inference across slices with 3D non-maximum suppression.
data_reading: Converts annotations to YOLO bounding box format
data_processing: Percentile-based intensity normalization for contrast enhancement
Extraction of multiple slices around annotated motors to capture 3D context
Tomogram-level train/validation split (80/20) to prevent leakage
ensembling: Applies 3D non-maximum suppression to cluster nearby 2D detections into single 3D motor locations during inference.
insights: Treating volumetric biological imaging as a 2D object detection task simplifies the pipeline while retaining accuracy.
Tomogram-level data splitting is critical to prevent information leakage between training and validation sets.
3D non-maximum suppression effectively clusters redundant 2D detections into single 3D motor locations.
